title_en: Hope for thousands of MS patients as 'life-changing' drug now on NHS in England
title_zh: 数千名多发性硬化症患者迎来希望：“改变生活”的药物在英格兰NHS上线
summary_zh: 一种名为法姆普利定（Fampridine）的药物即将在英格兰NHS提供，该药通过增强神经信号帮助多发性硬化症（MS）患者改善行走能力。此前该药因成本效益问题被英格兰NHS拒绝，但在威尔士、苏格兰和北爱尔兰已可用。每年约5000名符合条件的患者可受益。患者Aysen Slack曾自费使用，因费用过高而停止，现可重新尝试。卫生官员和MS协会表示，此药有望显著提升患者独立性和生活质量。
reading_passage:
Aysen Slack says she used to pay for the drug privately until it became too costly

The first drug to help people with multiple sclerosis walk is being made available on the NHS in England.

Fampridine boosts nerve signals to make it easier for some people to move and live independently.

Aysen Slack, who used to pay for fampridine privately until it became too costly, said the decision would make a "huge difference".

Up to 5,000 people in England could benefit each year. The drug is already available elsewhere in the UK.

Multiple sclerosis is caused by the immune system attacking the protective covering around nerve cells - known as myelin.

This damage impairs the ability of nerves to carry electrical messages from the brain to the rest of the body - including those needed for the precise control of muscles needed to walk.

Fampridine is known as a "signal booster" as it helps those electrical messages still get through.

Clinical trials showed around four in 10 people taking the drug had improvements in their walking speed and in how long they could walk for.

This could be the difference between getting around the house or needing help at home or enabling people to go out and see friends and family.

Current NHS support includes physiotherapy and walking aids.

The drug has previously been rejected for use by the NHS in England as it was not considered value for money, although it did become available in Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland.

Aysen Slack, 65 and from Eastbourne, chose to pay privately for the pills once she heard about them.

She said fampridine was "working well for me" but came at a "significant expense and I could not keep doing that forever" so she had to stop.

Slack said it was "great news" the drug was coming to the NHS and she "would definitely like to try" again.

"My mobility has decreased a lot and even in my flat I have to use sticks now," she said, "it would make a huge difference to my life if I were able to improve my walking."

More than 120,000 people live with MS in England. However, only around 5,000 people a year will be eligible - based on the extent of their walking difficulties.

Patients will try the medicine for up to a month and only those showing "clear benefit" will be able to continue taking it.

Prof Frankie Swords, the NHS national medical director, said: "Walking difficulties can have a huge impact on the freedom and independence of people with MS, so this signal-boosting pill could be life-changing for thousands of patients.

"Even a modest improvement could mean getting around the house more easily, going out with greater confidence and living more independently."

Ceri Smith, from the MS Society, said they were "delighted" by the news as the drug could be "life-changing" for many, allowing people to "live more independently or stay in employment".

vocabulary.example: Loud noise can impair your hearing over time.
Only students with high grades are eligible for the scholarship.
Regular exercise can help maintain mobility in old age.
There has been a modest rise in sales this quarter.
This restaurant offers great value for money with its generous portions.
Volunteering your time can make a real difference in the community.
The study demonstrated a clear benefit of the new treatment.
After her hip surgery, she was able to get around using a walker.
They love to go out to the cinema on weekends.
Scientists are studying the full extent of climate change.

difficult_sentences.sentence: This damage impairs the ability of nerves to carry electrical messages from the brain to the rest of the body - including those needed for the precise control of muscles needed to walk.
difficult_sentences.explanation_zh: 主句为“This damage impairs the ability”，后接of短语修饰ability，to carry...为不定式作后置定语说明ability的内容。破折号后的including...短语进一步举例说明，其中needed for...和needed to walk均为过去分词短语作后置定语修饰those和muscles。可借鉴此句式用including引出具体对象并用分词短语修饰。
difficult_sentences.grammar_point: 不定式作后置定语、现在分词短语作状语/定语、过去分词短语作定语
difficult_sentences.translation_zh: 这种损伤削弱了神经将电信号从大脑传递到身体其他部位的能力——包括那些精确控制行走所需肌肉的信号。

difficult_sentences.sentence: Aysen Slack, who used to pay for fampridine privately until it became too costly, said the decision would make a 'huge difference'.
difficult_sentences.explanation_zh: 本句包含一个非限制性定语从句“who used to...costly”修饰主语Aysen Slack，其中“until it became too costly”为时间状语从句。主句为“Aysen Slack said (that)...”，that引导的宾语从句省略了that。这种结构常用于补充人物背景信息。
difficult_sentences.grammar_point: 非限制性定语从句、时间状语从句、宾语从句
difficult_sentences.translation_zh: 艾森·斯拉克曾经自费购买法姆普利定，直到费用过高而停止，她说这一决定将带来“巨大的改变”。

difficult_sentences.sentence: The drug has previously been rejected for use by the NHS in England as it was not considered value for money, although it did become available in Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland.
difficult_sentences.explanation_zh: 主句为“The drug has previously been rejected”，之后“for use”表示原因，“as it was not considered value for money”为原因状语从句，最后“although”引导让步状语从句表示对比。did在让步从句中表示强调。可学习用although连接对比事实。
difficult_sentences.grammar_point: 现在完成时被动语态、原因状语从句、让步状语从句
difficult_sentences.translation_zh: 这种药此前曾被英格兰NHS因认为性价比不高而拒绝使用，尽管它在威尔士、苏格兰和北爱尔兰已经可以使用。

difficult_sentences.sentence: Even a modest improvement could mean getting around the house more easily, going out with greater confidence and living more independently.
difficult_sentences.explanation_zh: 本句主谓为“a modest improvement could mean”，宾语由三个并列的动名词短语担任：“getting around...”、“going out...”和“living...”，结构整齐，用逗号分隔。even修饰主语，强调轻微改善也能有显著意义。可模仿用动名词并列作宾语。
difficult_sentences.grammar_point: 动名词作宾语、并列结构、比较级修饰
difficult_sentences.translation_zh: 哪怕只是轻微的改善，也可能意味着在家中更自如地活动、更有信心地外出以及更独立地生活。

idioms.usage_note: Often used to emphasize a significant positive change.
idioms.example: A little encouragement can make a huge difference in a child's confidence.

idioms.meaning_zh: 信号增强器；这里比喻增强神经信号
idioms.usage_note: In this context, it's used metaphorically for the drug's function.
idioms.example: The Wi-Fi signal booster improved the network coverage in the building.

idioms.usage_note: Adjective describing something that dramatically alters a person's life.
idioms.example: Winning the scholarship was a life-changing opportunity for her.

idioms.usage_note: Indicates that something is effective or suitable for someone.
idioms.example: This study method works well for students who are visual learners.

reading_questions.question: What is the main news reported in the article?

options.A: Fampridine has been approved for NHS use in England after previous rejection.
options.B: Multiple sclerosis is a debilitating disease affecting nerve signals.
options.C: Aysen Slack is a patient who can now afford her medication.
options.D: Clinical trials show walking improvements in MS patients.

reading_questions.question: What did Aysen Slack do before fampridine became available on the NHS in England?

options.A: She paid for the drug out of pocket until it became too expensive.
options.B: She received the drug for free through a clinical trial.
options.C: She moved to Scotland to access the drug.
options.D: She relied on physiotherapy and walking aids.

reading_questions.question: What can be inferred about Aysen Slack’s response to the NHS decision?

options.A: She is cautiously hopeful, as she would like to try the drug again.
options.B: She is indifferent since she has moved to using sticks.
options.C: She plans to seek treatment in Northern Ireland instead.
options.D: She believes the drug will cure her mobility issues permanently.

reading_questions.question: The word 'boosts' in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to:
What is the author's attitude toward the NHS decision?

options.A: Neutral, presenting facts and quotes from various sources.
options.B: Critical of the NHS for delaying the drug.
options.D: Skeptical, highlighting the limited number of eligible patients.

reading_questions.question: Which of the following best describes the organization of the article?

options.A: A chronological history of MS treatments leading to fampridine.
options.B: A problem-solution structure: MS walking difficulties and the drug's mechanism.
options.C: A comparative analysis of healthcare systems across the UK.
options.D: Announcement of a policy change, supported by patient story, trial evidence, and expert comment.

reading_questions.question: Which of the following would be the most appropriate title for this article?

options.A: "MS Society Delighted with New Drug Approval"
options.B: "Fampridine: A Miracle Cure for All MS Patients"
options.C: "How Aysen Slack Overcame MS with a New Drug"
options.D: "NHS England Finally Allows Fampridine for MS Walking Difficulties"

sources.title: Hope for thousands of MS patients as 'life-changing' drug now on NHS in England
